coredata.dc:title: Preparation of lignin-based full-color carbon quantum dots and their multifunctionalization with waterborne polyurethanes
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jianfeng
Ma, Xingyuan
coredata.dc:description: Lignin is a popular material for energy transition and high-value utilization due to its low cost, non-toxicity, renewability, and widespread availability. However, its complex structure has hindered its application. Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) uses water as a dispersion medium, which is safer for humans and the environment but also leads to disadvantages such as poor mechanical properties and water resistance. In this study, we prepared multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a wide range of wavelengths from lignin. We successfully prepared panchromatic CQDs by additive mixing. The redshift of the emission wavelength is attributed to the synergistic effect of the sp2 conjugated structure and the surface functional groups. The full-color solid-state luminescence of the CQDs was successfully achieved, and most importantly, the application of full-color CQDs in light-emitting diodes was realized. Moreover, the embedding of the multicolor CQDs in WPU not only makes WPU luminescent but also improves the water resistance and mechanical properties of WPUs. The hydrogen-bonding interactions between the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs and the urethane were responsible for the high performance of the composite. We investigated the UV and strong blue light shielding abilities of WPU/yellow CQDs films, which resulted from the unique absorption peaks of yellow CQDs in the UV region and the strong blue light region. This work provides an efficient method for the high-value utilization of biomass materials and paves the way for the multifunctional application of WPU.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Waterborne polyurethane
Solid state luminescence
Light emitting diode
Shielding performance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of full-color CQDs/SiO2 phosphors and full-color CQDs/WPU fluorescent films
Preparation of UV and intense blue light shielding WPU films
Characterization of physicochemical properties of CQDs
Characterization of optical properties of CQDs
Possible emission and formation mechanism of CQDs
Preparation of full-color CQDs and their optical performance applications
Multi-color WPU@CQDs composite films for multifunctional applications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Diagram of the synthesis strategy for multicolor CQDs.
TEM and HRTEM images of (a) B-CQDs, (b) G-CQDs, (c) Y-CQDs and (d) R-CQDs. Particle size distributions of (e) B-CQDs, (f) G-CQDs, (g) Y-CQDs and (h) R-CQDs.
XPS patterns of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs.

Absorption spectra, excitation spectra, and emission spectra of (a) B-CQDs, (b) G-CQDs, (c) Y-CQDs and (d)R-CQDs; Time-resolved fluorescence decay curve of (e) B-CQDs, (f) G-CQDs, (g) Y-CQDs and (h)R-CQDs.
Schematic representation of possible formation mechanisms and energy levels of CQDs with tunable PL emission.

(a) Fluorescent and physical images of full-color CQDs; (b) fluorescence spectra of full-color CQDs (O-CQDs, V-CQDs and W-CQDs with 365 nm as excitation wavelength); (c) CIE1931 coordinate image of full-color CQDs.
Full color CQDs/SiO2 phosphor and full color CQDs/WPU fluorescent film physical and fluorescent diagrams.
(a–g) Emission spectra of full-color CQDs-based LEDs; (inset) corresponding optical photographs; (h–n) CIE color coordinates of full-color CQDs-based LEDs.

(a) Normalized fluorescence emission pattern of four CQDs and four WPU@CQDs composite films; (b) stress-strain curves of four WPU@CQDs composite films; (c) four WPU@CQDs composite membranes water resistance curves; (d) four WPU@CQDs composite films TGA profiles; (e) four WPU@CQDs composite films DTG profiles; (f) schematic diagram of hydrogen bonding between CQDs and WPU; (g) UV absorption spectra of WPU and WPU@Y-CQDs-1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6; (h) UV transmission spectra of WPU and WPU@Y-CQDs-1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6; (i) shielding spectra of WPU and WPU@CQDs-1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 for UV (350 nm), intense blue light (425 nm) and visible light (550 nm).

head.ce:title: Preparation of lignin-based full-color carbon quantum dots and their multifunctionalization with waterborne polyurethanes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xian, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignin is a popular material for energy transition and high-value utilization due to its low cost, non-toxicity, renewability, and widespread availability. However, its complex structure has hindered its application. Waterborne polyurethane (WPU) uses water as a dispersion medium, which is safer for humans and the environment but also leads to disadvantages such as poor mechanical properties and water resistance. In this study, we prepared multicolor photoluminescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a wide range of wavelengths from lignin. We successfully prepared panchromatic CQDs by additive mixing. The redshift of the emission wavelength is attributed to the synergistic effect of the sp2 conjugated structure and the surface functional groups. The full-color solid-state luminescence of the CQDs was successfully achieved, and most importantly, the application of full-color CQDs in light-emitting diodes was realized. Moreover, the embedding of the multicolor CQDs in WPU not only makes WPU luminescent but also improves the water resistance and mechanical properties of WPUs. The hydrogen-bonding interactions between the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs and the urethane were responsible for the high performance of the composite. We investigated the UV and strong blue light shielding abilities of WPU/yellow CQDs films, which resulted from the unique absorption peaks of yellow CQDs in the UV region and the strong blue light region. This work provides an efficient method for the high-value utilization of biomass materials and paves the way for the multifunctional application of WPU.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional fluorescent nanomaterials (semiconductor quantum dots, chalcogenides, etc.) are receiving more and more attention due to their high quantum yield, good photostability, and process controllability [1,2]. However, most of them contain heavy metals, resulting in a certain degree of toxicity, which seriously limits the development of fluorescent nanomaterials. There is an urgent need to develop new types of fluorescent nanomaterials that are safe, environmentally friendly, affordable, and sustainable [3,4].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of carbon nanomaterial with adjustable photoluminescent properties. They have become increasingly important in many fields due to their biocompatibility, non-toxicity, good water solubility, and eco-friendliness [5]. So far, producing CQDs that exhibit a high quantum yield with full-color emission is still a significant challenge [6]. The inability to produce high-quality, full-color CQDs severely limits their potential applications in fluorescence imaging, optoelectronic devices, anti-counterfeiting, and energy conversion [7–9]. Currently, the bottom-up method for CQD preparation mainly uses organic small-molecule compounds and biomass materials as raw materials. While research on the former is well-established, the latter has mainly focused on using high-value food products for CQD preparation [10–12]. The performance of CQDs is closely related to the structure of raw materials and the preparation methods [8,13].Various vegetables (such as lentils, parsnips, sweet potatoes, garlic, cucumbers, and yams) containing numerous nutrients and minerals, as well as fruits (such as sugarcane, bananas, apples, and grapefruit) containing a diverse range of vitamins and sugars, have been used to produce CQDs with different functionalities. Additionally, starch, milk, ginseng, coffee, and green tea have been utilized as carbon sources for CQD preparation [12,14–16]. However, there is still a lack of methods to produce full-color CQDs from low-cost or waste biomass materials to achieve high-value utilization of biomass resources.

To align with the global theme of energy conservation and environmental protection and achieve sustainable development, it is essential to choose proper raw materials for the preparation of CQDs that are non-toxic, harmless, renewable, and resource-recyclable [17]. Lignin is one of the biomass materials with the largest stock in the world, which mainly exists in the waste materials of the paper industry. Its huge stock, easy availability, low cost, and sustainable utilization have been highly expected in the context of the energy transition, but due to its complex structural composition, the utilization of lignin was difficult [18]. Lignin is one of the best carbon sources for the preparation of CQDs because of its rich conjugated structure, and the oxygen-containing functional groups in its structure create unique advantages for the formation of conjugated carbon nuclei and various surface states [19].

Waterborne polyurethanes (WPUs) are a class of polymeric materials with optimal film-forming properties prepared by replacing organic solvents with water and have been used in a wide range of applications such as glass, paper, leather, and coatings [20–22]. Although the use of water instead of toxic solvents has made the development of WPU brighter, it has also led to problems such as poor thermal properties, mechanical properties, and water resistance, which have seriously limited its application [23]. Embedding nanomaterials into the molecular chain of WPU is the best way to solve this development bottleneck [24]. However, the combination of nanomaterials with WPUs faces multiple challenges. Firstly, the agglomeration of nanomaterials leads to serious damage to the basic properties of WPU. Secondly, the single structure and lack of functionalization of conventional nanomaterials, most notably the lack of interaction sites with WPU, lead to poor performance of modified WPU [25]. Although various chemical additives have been developed at present to solve the above problems, the cumbersome process, increased cost, and long process are not conducive to the good development of WPU [26]. Innovative work is needed in this field to solve the two important problems in the modification of WPU with nanomaterials. The structure of CQDs is usually regarded as a conjugated carbon core embedded in an amorphous shell, and its surface contains numerous hydrophilic functional groups, which not only enable CQDs to be uniformly dispersed in WPU but also interact with carbamate through hydrogen bonding correlation to achieve multi-functionalization of WPU without chemical additives [27].

Herein, we prepared full-color CQDs from the biomass material lignin, and the red-shift of the emission wavelength was mainly due to the synergistic effect of the sp2 conjugated structure and the surface functional groups. To realize the solid-state luminescence of CQDs, we uniformly dispersed the CQDs into SiO2 and WPU, and successfully realized the phosphor and fluorescent film with the full-color CQDs. By adding the CQDs into WPU, the abundant functional groups of CQDs bind to WPU through hydrogen bonding, which greatly improves the inherent defects of WPU with poor water resistance and mechanical properties. Due to the high compatibility between CQDs and WPU, as well as the unique absorption peaks of yellow CQDs (Y-CQDs) in the region of strong blue light and ultraviolet light, WPU@Y-CQDs composite films with high shielding ability for strong blue light and ultraviolet light and high transmittance for visible light have been successfully prepared.

Alkaline lignin and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were purchased from Tianjin Huasheng Reagent Co., Ltd.; ethanol, ethylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine, and Nile Blue were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd.; waterborne polyurethane was selected as polycarbonate type (H909), purchased from Luoyang Shuo Hao Environmental Protection New Material Co., Ltd.; all chemicals were not processed for further analysis.

Full-color CQDs were mainly prepared for doping of lignin by three different nitrogen sources to obtain blue, green, yellow, and red, and by additive mixing to obtain orange, violet, and white. Lignin 0.1 g was taken and added to 20 ml of ethanol, ultrasonically dispersed for 30 min, kept at 200 °C for 5 h in a hydrothermal reactor, cooled to room temperature, and then the crude product of the reaction was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min. The crude product was taken out, and the supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h (1000 Da, deionized water exchanged once in 24 h), freeze-dried, and dissolved in ethanol to prepare blue CQDs (B-CQDs) for the next step of characterization and analysis [28,29]. The green CQDs (G-CQDs) were prepared by changing the reaction solvent to water and the reaction materials to 0.1 g of lignin and 0.1 g of ethylenediamine; the yellow CQDs (Y-CQDs) were prepared by adding only 0.1 g of o-phenylenediamine compared to the B-CQDs; and the red CQDs (R-CQDs) were prepared by adding only 0.05 g of Nile blue compared to the B-CQDs. Orange CQDs (O-CQDs) were prepared by mixing Y-CQDs and R-CQDs; violet CQDs (V-CQDs) were prepared by mixing B-CQDs and R-CQDs; and white CQDs (W-CQDs) were prepared by mixing B-CQDs, G-CQDs, and R-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of full-color CQDs/SiO2 phosphors and full-color CQDs/WPU fluorescent films
ce:section.ce:para: Seven prepared CQDs were prepared by dispersing 2 mg of each in 10 ml of ethanol, adding 10 g of SiO2 powder for column chromatography, and ultrasonication for 2 h to homogeneously disperse the CQDs, then drying at 70 °C for 2 h [30]. 10 g of WPU was added to a solution of CQDs at the same concentration as the prepared phosphor, sonicated and dispersed for 30 min, poured into an evaporating dish, and left to stand for 24 h before being kept in an oven at 130 °C for 2 h [31].
Prepare 2 ml of each of the CQDs solutions in 2.3 by adding 1 g of PVP to it, mix well to form a gel, suspend the droplets on a 365 nm UV chip, and dry at room temperature for 24 h [30].

The addition of 5 ml of an ethanol solution of X-CQDs at a concentration of 1 mg/ml to 10 g of WPU (30 % solids) was dispersed by ultrasonication for 30 min. It was then allowed to set for 24 h and held at 130 °C for 2 h (X = B, G, Y, and R). Four WPU@CQDs composite films (0.167 wt%) were successfully prepared [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of UV and intense blue light shielding WPU films
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the shielding performance of WPU@Y-CQDs against UV and strong blue light, gradient experiments were carried out. 0.01 g of Y-CQDs was dissolved in 25 ml of ethanol to obtain 25 ml of Y-CQDs solution at a concentration of 0.4 mg/ml. 1 ml of the prepared Y-CQDs solution was added to 5 g of polycarbonate-based waterborne polyurethane (30 % solids content) to obtain The membranes WPU@Y-CQDs-1 (0.027 wt%) were prepared by adding 0 ml, 2 ml, 3 ml, 4 ml, 5 ml, and 6 ml of Y-CQDs solution, corresponding to WPU, WPU@Y-CQDs-2 (0.053 wt%), WPU@Y-CQDs-3 (0.08 wt%), WPU@Y-CQDs-4 (0.106 wt%), WPU@Y-CQDs-5 (0.133 wt%), and WPU@Y-CQDs-6 (0.16 wt%). heated at 130 °C for 2 h [21].

Crystallographic properties of CDs were measured with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D/max2200PC, Japan) in the range of 10–60° at room temperature. Functional groups of CDs were detected with a Fourier transform infrared instrument (FT-IR, Vertex70, German) in the spectral range of 4000–500 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific) was performed with an Al Kα source. All samples were placed on a Si plate for the XPS test. The morphology of the samples was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM 2100 F) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). UV absorption and transmission were performed by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Cary 5000). Fluorescence properties were performed by fluorescence spectroscopy (FS5, Edinburgh, UK). Thermal properties of the composite films were analyzed by a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA-DTG, DISCOVERY, Germany). The mechanical properties of the composite films were carried out by a servo material multifunctional high and low temperature control tester (Gauthier, Dongguan). All optical data were normalized and characterized in a four-way quartz cuvette at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of physicochemical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the process diagram for the preparation of multicolored CQDs via lignin with three nitrogen-containing compounds. To study the formation process and various optical properties of the CQDs, the physicochemical properties of the four CQDs were characterized and investigated. Fig. 2 (a–d) shows the TEM and HRTEM (inset) of the four CQDs, and (e–h) shows the corresponding particle size distributions of the CQDs. It can be observed that the four CQDs (B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs) showed uniform monodisperse distributions in an ethanol solution, with average particle sizes of 1.82, 1.90, 2.11 and 2.35 nm, respectively. The smaller particle size and the gradually increasing particle size distribution not only prove that these four CQDs are at the quantum level but also indicate that the particle size effect is very important for the tunable photoluminescence (PL) properties of CQDs [32]. As can be seen from the HRTEM images, all the CQDs show similar and clear lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphitic carbon [33]. The results suggest that the nucleation and growth size of CQDs may be related to the nitrogen-containing compounds used in the synthesis process.

The graphitization degree of the four CQDs was characterized by XRD patterns for a better understanding of the emission mechanism of the CQDs and the effect of crystallinity on the CQDs. As shown in Fig. S1, the B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs all show broad diffraction bands centered at about 22.3°, corresponding to the (100) crystalline plane of the graphite structure [34]. Importantly, the intensity is enhanced from the B-CQDs to the R-CQDs, suggesting that the degree of graphitization gradually increases with the redshift of the emission wavelength. For these four CQDs, the gradual increase in the degree of graphitization suggests that the increase in conjugated domain size and sp2 carbon content leads to a narrowing of the energy band gap and a redshift in the emission wavelength [35]. The TEM and XRD results are in relative agreement, suggesting that the quantum size effect is the main factor affecting the redshift in the emission wavelength.

Four CQDs were investigated for their surface functional groups and chemical compositions using FT-IR and XPS. As shown in Fig. S2, all the CQDs have obvious absorption peaks at 1710 cm−1, which mainly corresponds to the CO stretching vibration absorption peaks, and the absorption peaks of B-CQDs are especially obvious, which may be due to the fact that amide bonding cannot be generated without nitrogen doping, resulting in the majority of the CO and OH in lignin attaching to the surface of CQDs [27,36]. Absorption peaks at 3300 cm−1 for all CQDs correspond to OH stretching vibrational absorption peaks. The absorption peak at 1545 cm−1 of the four CQDs is mainly due to the CC stretching vibration in the benzene ring structure [31,37]. From G-CQDs to R-CQDs showed a strong absorption peak at 3650 cm−1, which was mainly led by the NH stretching vibration, and the absorption intensity gradually increased with the redshift of the emission wavelength, indicating a gradual increase in the NH content [27]. In summary, it is shown that all four CQDs contain abundant functional groups such as OH/NH and CO on their surfaces.

In Fig. 3(i), the three typical peaks at 284.8, 399.2, and 531.3 eV for G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs belong to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively, and the absorption peaks of B-CQDs at 284.8 and 531.3 eV correspond to C1s and O1s, demonstrating that three different nitrogen-containing complexes were successfully doped into the structure of CQDs, and the nitrogen content gradually increased with the redshift of the emission wavelength (Table S1). The deconvolution of the C1s spectra of the four CQDs into three components, i.e., C-C/C=C (284.6 eV), C-O/C-N (285.8 eV), and C=O/COOH (287.8 eV), is shown in Fig. 3(a–d), and with the redshift of emission wavelengths, the C=O/COOH content of the sp2 structure gradually increases (Table S2) [30,38]; The deconvolution of the O1s spectra of the four CQDs, shown in Fig. 3(e–h), displays two peaks near 531.2 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to CO and CO, which are very similar to the C1s spectra, with an overall gradual increase in the CO content (Table S3) [29]; Fig. 3(j–l) shows that the deconvolution of the N1s spectra of G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs showed two peaks near 398.8 and 399.6 eV, corresponding to pyridine N and amide N [32]. Increased levels of amide N and decreased levels of pyridine N lead to exciton trapping and changes in the surface state, resulting in a redshift (Table S3) [39]. The XPS results are basically consistent with the FT-IR results that all CQDs basically have these functional groups on their surfaces, but their contents show obvious differences. Different surface functional group contents determine different electronic domains, which seriously affect the electron-leaping situation and determine the fluorescence color [40]. Gradually increasing the contents of CO and amide N from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, and varying the contents of functional groups on the surface of CQDs led to the formation of different surface states, which seriously affected the fluorescence emission of CQDs [32,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of optical properties of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the photophysical properties of the four CQDs, they were characterized and analyzed using UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy. The normalized UV, excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of the four CQDs are shown in Fig. 4 (a–d), and the embedded image in the upper right corner is a picture of the four CQDs solutions in practice with the 365 nm UV lamp turned off and on. It can be clearly observed from the figure that the UV curves of all CQDs have strong absorption peaks at 230–280 nm, which is mainly due to the π-π*/n-π* electronic leaps of the C=C/C=N bond in the structure [19]. In addition, for the long-wavelength emitting CQDs (Y-CQDs and R-CQDs), there are relatively strong absorption peaks at 425 and 550 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* leaps in the more sp2 (C=O) structures in Y-CQDs and R-CQDs [32]. More importantly, its Ex and Em are in relative coincidence with the absorption peaks at 425 and 550 nm, implying that the Em of yellow and red light originates from the leaps of the absorption bands at 425 and 550 nm and is not related to the absorption peaks below 300 nm [42]. Most importantly, Y-CQDs have perfect characteristic absorption peaks in the strong blue light (400–450 nm) and UV region (<400 nm), which provides an inherent advantage for their functionalized applications.

Under the optimal Ex wavelength excitation, the optimal Em of the four CQDs were 480, 515, 545, and 635 nm, respectively. To better understand the surface structure of the CQDs, the Em of the four CQDs was characterized under different Ex (Fig. S3). B-CQDs and G-CQDs showed Ex dependence, and Y-CQDs and R-CQDs exhibited an Ex-independent, which indicated that multiple functional groups formed different surface states on the surface of the CQDs, resulting in PL generation from different surface domains; Y-CQDs and R-CQDs have homogeneous surface structural domains, and their Em wavelengths coincide with the redshift of Ex, which indicated that the PL generation from different surface domains was caused by the formation of different surface states [43,44]. Absolute photoluminescence quantum yields of 0.22,4.26,6.52 and 47.9 % were determined using the integral sphere method for the four B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs, respectively (Fig. S4). The average fluorescence lifetimes of the four CQDs fitted with a double exponential function are shown in Fig. 4(e–h) and Table S4, which are 4.88, 5.82, 7.05, and 4.31 ns, respectively. The double exponential function fit consists of a short-lived fraction τ1 and a long-lived fraction τ2 for the recombination process in the core and surface states, respectively. The proportion of τ1 increases from 41.10 % to 97.13 % with the redshift of the Em wavelength, further indicating that the conjugated sp2 structure determines the fluorescence properties [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible emission and formation mechanism of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: By analyzing the characterization results of CQDs, the reasons for the redshift of the Em wavelength of CQDs and the possible formation mechanism of lignin-based CQDs were explored. During the preparation of CQDs by the hydrothermal/thermal solvent method, the precursors underwent intense dehydration, condensation, and carbonization reactions under high temperatures and pressure. The lignin structure contains a large number of benzene ring structures and oxygen-containing functional groups, and no nitrogen addition is needed in the preparation of B-CQDs; the Em wavelength shifted from the blue range to the green range with the addition of the simple nitrogen-containing compound ethylenediamine; the Em wavelength shifted to the yellow range with the addition of o-phenylenediamine (which contains nitrogen and benzene ring structures); and the Em wavelength shifted to the red range with the addition of Nile blue (which contains a large number of benzene ring structures and also contains nitrogen). The Em wavelength moves to the red range. XPS, FT-IR, and TEM analyses showed a gradual increase in the size of the four CQDs with the redshift of the Em wavelength and a gradual increase in the N content (increase in amide N content and decrease in pyridine N content) and CO content. During the reaction process, nitrogen-containing compounds containing larger benzene ring structures were introduced into the lignin structure, which not only enlarged the sp2 conjugate size but also doped the N element into the CQDs structure, causing them to undergo amidation reactions and form various surface states on their surfaces, which resulted in a narrowing of the energy band gap and a redshift of the Em wavelength. Therefore, the redshift of the Em wavelength of CQDs is attributed to the synergistic effects of the sp2 conjugation structure and surface states.

Theoretical calculations were carried out to verify the above conclusions. From the UV absorption spectra of the four CQDs, it can be found that the absorption ranges of the four CQDs gradually become broader with the redshift of the Em wavelength, and thus their energy band gaps were calculated by using Tauc's formula (Eg(opt) = 1240 / λ edge , where λ edge is the wavelength corresponding to the absorption peak of the largest exciton) [27]. The calculated results are shown in Fig. S5, where the energy bandgap gradually narrows with the redshift of the Em wavelength, and the energy bandgaps of the B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and R-CQDs are 2.60, 2.41, 2.27, and 1.96 eV, respectively. This is consistent with the results of the sp2 conjugate size effect. Decreasing the pyridine N content may lead to the exciton jump and the change of the surface states, which leads to the fluorescence redshift [39,45]. Increasing the amide N and CO content not only enlarges the sp2 conjugated structure domain and decreases the Eg, but also forms different degrees of surface oxidation on the surface of the CQDs, which leads to the redshift of the Em [19,46,47]. Based on the rich variety of surface states on the surface of CQDs, different energy levels and different energies released by nonradiative diffraction of excitons lead to fluorescence Em of different colors [48,49]. Therefore, it can be determined that the red shift of the Em wavelength is attributed to the sp2 conjugate size effect and the synergistic effect of the surface states. The possible formation processes and energy level structures of the four CQDs are shown in Fig. 5 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of full-color CQDs and their optical performance applications
ce:section.ce:para: Four color CQDs were prepared by specific experimental conditions, and to widen the application of optical properties of CQDs, we prepared O-CQDs, V-CQDs, and W-CQDs by additive mixing, as shown in the physical and fluorescence diagrams in Fig. 6(a), where B-CQDs, G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, O-CQDs, R-CQDs, V-CQDs, and W-CQDs were prepared, respectively from a–g. The normalized fluorescence spectra are shown in Fig. 6(b), and all three CQDs prepared by additive mixing have significant Em peaks at specific positions. The CIE1931 coordinates of the seven CQDs are plotted in Fig. 6(c), and it can be clearly observed that the fluorescence colors of all seven CQDs are in the corresponding spectral regions.

The solid-state luminescence of CQDs has been realized in two ways: by overcoming the self-burst phenomenon of CQDs caused by resonance energy transfer due to aggregation or direct π-π interactions [50]. First of all, the high-quality adsorbent material SiO2 (silica gel column for column chromatography) with high specific surface area and porosity was chosen as the dispersing medium, whose excellent adsorbability with the abundant functional groups on the surface of CQDs through hydrogen bonding helped realize the good luminescence of the CQDs-based phosphors. Another option is to choose the aqueous system of polyurethane as the dispersing substrate, as the many hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of CQDs provided an excellent advantage for the dispersion and bonding of CQDs in aqueous materials, thereby achieving a good CQDs-based phosphor film. The two forms of CQDs-based solid-state luminescence are shown in Fig. 7 .

Finally, we used a 365 nm chip as the excitation source and coated the UV chip with the above PVP/CQDs composites to obtain seven corresponding bright blue, green, yellow, orange, red, violet, and white LEDs, with the Em spectra and optical photographs (inset) shown in Fig. 8 (a–g) and their CIE coordinates shown in Fig. 8(h–n). Characteristic Em peaks can be clearly observed in the Em spectra of O-CQDs, V-CQDs, and W-CQDs-based LEDs. Especially for the W-CQD-based LEDs, significant emission peaks (corresponding to 460, 510, and 630 nm, respectively) can be clearly observed in the 365 nm Ex of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, and R-CQDs, which indicates that we have successfully used the three primary colors mixed to prepare cool white light (CRI = 89.6 and CCT = 7631 K). Both applications show that the fluorescent CQDs prepared by reusing lignin are promising in numerous fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-color WPU@CQDs composite films for multifunctional applications
ce:section.ce:para: WPU is a widely used polymer material with the advantages of being non-toxic, eco-friendly, and easy to form films, especially with water as the dispersing medium, and the urethane groups contained in its structure provide carriers and binding sites for the anti-aggregation and binding of CQDs. Firstly, the normalized Em spectra of four composite films of WPU@CQDs are shown in Fig. 9(a), and the results show a complete overlap with the optimal Em spectra of CQDs, which indicates that the WPU as a film-forming material has a good dispersion of CQDs and does not cause a color shift of CQDs. WPU expands chains and emulsifies in water, resulting in its lack of mechanical properties. This problem was solved by adding functional nanomaterial CQDs without additives, and the stress-strain curves of WPU and four WPU/CQDs membranes are shown in Fig. 9(b). The stress-strain of the WPU membrane was 19.8 MPa and 201.6 %, respectively, and with the addition of B-, G-, Y- and R-CQDs, the stresses were 23.5,24.9, 26.7 and 28.7 MPa, respectively, with the maximum stress elevated by 44.9 %; the strains of the WPU@B-CQDs and the WPU@G-CQDs were all around 250 %; the WPU@Y-CQDs decreased and were 224.3 %; and the WPU@R-CQDs reached the maximum and were 273.1 %, with the maximum strain elevated by 35.5 %. Good mechanical properties and flexibility make the WPU@R-CQDs have a wider range of applications and higher application values.

WPU is widely used in coating materials because of its excellent film-forming properties. We have already studied its mechanical properties as mentioned above, but water resistance is still crucial for coating materials, and poor water resistance will lead to color change, softening, adhesion failure, blistering, and even coating film rupture [22]. The related water resistance results are analyzed in Fig. 9(c), where the absorption of the membranes showed a rising trend in the first 60 h. The addition of CQDs greatly improved the water resistance of the WPU membranes; most notably, the WPU@R-CQDs reduced the water absorption of the WPUs from 0.049 % to 0.026 %, which increased the water resistance of the WPUs by 46.9 %.

To further investigate the effect of CQDs on various properties of WPU, TGA-DTG of WPU and WPU@CQDs were tested, as shown in Fig. 9(d) and (e), from which it can be observed that the test results of the five membranes are basically the same. The results are basically the same as our expectations, and the mass fraction is <2 ‰, which makes it difficult to affect the thermal performance of WPU film. Based on the above four characterization analyses, adding CQDs to WPU not only does not cause fluorescence burst and shift but also does not affect the film formation of WPU. Furthermore, the solid-state luminescence of CQDs is achieved and fluorescence is given to the WPU film. More importantly, the mechanical properties and water resistance of the WPU film are greatly improved. By analyzing, we speculate that this is the effect of hydrogen bonding between CQDs and multiple WPU molecular chains, which consumes part of the energy due to hydrogen bonding and the energy consumed by intertwining and entanglements of the molecular chains during the stretching process, enhancing the mechanical properties of the WPU film; Simultaneously, the closer bonding of the molecular chains improves the degree of crystallinity, which prevents water molecules from immersing and improves water resistance [21,51]. Fig. 9(f) shows the schematic diagram of hydrogen bonding between CQDs and WPU. The introduction of CQDs into WPU not only solves the inherent defects of WPU but also paves the way for the functionalization of WPU.

With the gradual warming of the global climate and the increasing destruction of the ozone layer, the Earth's surface is exposed to increasing intensities of ultraviolet and intense blue light. Intense ultraviolet light causes serious deterioration and irreparable damage to the human body and some light-sensitive products [52]. The popularization and circulation of various electronic products with strong blue light and small amounts of ultraviolet light produced by the screen have seriously led to anxiety, depression, and other serious adverse reactions [51]. Y-CQDs have obvious absorption peaks in the strong blue light region (400–450 nm) and the UV region (<400 nm), which indicates that Y-CQDs have good shielding performance against UV and strong blue light. Based on the study of modified WPU with multicolor CQDs, we prepared WPU@Y-CQDs with different gradients to investigate the shielding performance of the films against strong blue light and UV light.

Absorbance and transmittance of the gradient WPU@Y-CQDs film for intense blue and UV light are shown in Fig. 9(g) and (h). It can be clearly observed that the transmittance of the full UV region and the strong blue light region decreases with the addition of Y-CQDs. When the amount of Y-CQDs is 0.16 wt%, it can realize the shielding effect of most of the UV and strong blue light, and only slightly affect the visible light. Therefore, WPU@Y-CQDs-6 can efficiently realize the shielding of all UV and strong blue light. The comparative transmittance of visible light (550 nm), intense blue light (425 nm), and UV light (350 nm) by different gradient WPU@Y-CQDs is shown in Fig. 9(i). WPU@Y-CQDs-6 is very effective in blocking 93.1 % of intense UV light and 85.5 % of intense blue light while providing high transmission of 77.3 % of visible light. To further explore the reliability for practical applications, the stability of the WPU@Y-CQDs-6 membrane was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S6. The excellent UV and intense blue light shielding was always maintained under 36 h long-term irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp in the laboratory. This indicates that the WPU@Y-CQDs membrane has very reliable applicability to intense blue light and UV light.

In conclusion, multicolor CQDs were prepared by using the biomass material lignin and different nitrogen-containing compounds, and full-color CQDs were prepared by additive mixing. The solid-state luminescence of CQDs was successfully achieved by combining the CQDs with SiO2 and WPU, and the great potential application of CQDs in light-emitting diodes (LEDs) was demonstrated based on the excellent fluorescence properties of CQDs. Combining the advantages of WPU with water as the dispersing medium, CQDs with hydrophilic functional groups are perfectly embedded into the molecular chain of WPU, which not only endows WPU with fluorescent properties but also solves the major defects of WPU such as poor water resistance and mechanical properties, which is of great significance for WPU as a functional coating material. Finally, the shielding performance of WPU@Y-CQDs against strong blue light and ultraviolet light was investigated with the help of the unique absorption peaks of Y-CQDs in the strong blue light and ultraviolet light regions. In summary, we have successfully used biomass materials to realize the development concept of energy saving and environmental protection, and at the same time, we have transformed the waste into high-value added and multi-purpose functional nanomaterials (CQDs) and combined them with WPUs to realize the multifunctional application of WPUs.
Jianfeng Li: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Xingyuan Ma: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the Fujian Bureau of Major Projects (2019H4018) funding.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.130860.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solvent-free rapid synthesis of carbon nanodots at atmospheric pressure: preparation of transparent light conversion films with ultrahigh efficient UV and blue light shielding
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gong, Xiao, et al. "Solvent-free rapid synthesis of carbon nanodots at atmospheric pressure: Preparation of transparent light conversion films with ultrahigh efficient UV and blue light shielding." Carbon. 210 (2023): 118044. doi:10.1016/j.carbon.2023.118044.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot/cellulose-based transparent films for efficient UV and high-energy blue light screening
ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Barman, Barun Kumar, et al. "Carbon dot/cellulose-based transparent films for efficient UV and high-energy blue light screening." ACS Sustainable Chemistry & Engineering. 9(29) (2021) 9879-9890. doi:10.1021/acssuschemeng.1c02791.
